nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and outer-reef habitats, typically encountered at depths of roughly 0–25 m, usually solitary or in pairs

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on benthic invertebrates and zooplankton. In captivity is generally fed a varied diet of meaty marine fare such as finely chopped marine fish, crustacean meats, mysis shrimp, and vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, complemented by algae-based foods. Feeding two or more times daily is commonly recommended, and vitamin supplementation has been reported to help maintain coloration.

imageCandidates.notes: Hectonichus, 1267x949. Sharp, well-lit side profile of a single live specimen showing the diagnostic two-tone grey-brown body, dark eye bar, dark rear-flank blotch, white dorsal margin and yellow soft-fin/tail base. Best overall ID and quality.
Debra Baker, 2048x1536. Crisp full-body side profile in situ; correct coloration, eye bar, yellow caudal-peduncle and white dorsal edge. Slightly dimmer lighting than the recommended pick.
Eric Knight, 1920x1080. In-situ lagoon habitat shot showing the species over sand near reef; correctly ID'd but the fish is small/distant, so kept only as habitat context.

summary: The Tahitian Butterflyfish (Chaetodon trichrous) is a rarely imported species endemic to French Polynesia. It carries a two-tone grey-and-brown body with a dark vertical bar through the eye and is generally considered intermediate in care. It is not reef-safe and does best in a mature fish-only system.
careNotes:
The Tahitian Butterflyfish is endemic to French Polynesia (Society, Tahiti, and Tuamotu Islands) and is only rarely available in the marine aquarium trade. It is generally considered a peaceful species that tends to do well in a mature FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) aquarium with non-aggressive tankmates, though it has been reported to become territorial if housed in too small a system.

This species is best kept singly or as a mated pair; pairs are generally advised to be introduced to the aquarium simultaneously to reduce aggression.

Water quality should be kept stable. Like other butterflyfishes, this species can be susceptible to HLLE (head and lateral line erosion, a condition that causes pitting and discoloration along the head and lateral line) when kept in poor conditions or fed an insufficiently varied diet, so vitamin-enriched and algae-based foods are generally advised alongside meaty fare.

Captive breeding has not been reliably documented. Like other Chaetodon species, it is reported to be oviparous (egg-scattering rather than live-bearing) and to form pairs during spawning.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and to form pairs during the breeding season; spawning has been documented in the wild but the species has not been reliably bred in captivity under typical hobby conditions.

additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden; swapped to FishBase, Quality Marine and fishy.wiki for cross-verification per playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12.0 cm TL), distribution (Society Islands, Tahiti, Tuamotu Islands), depth range (0–25 m), taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), and reproduction (oviparous, pairs).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperament, diet, reef safety and water parameters. Tank size listed as 55 gallons (~208 L) vs LiveAquaria's 70 gallons; averaged and biased upward to 300 L per tank-size special rule.

additional.name: fishy.wiki
additional.notes: Third cross-source for water parameters and adult size (12.7 cm). Recommends 264 L minimum and notes the species requires ample swimming space.

fish.breedingNotes: Reported oviparous and to form pairs during spawning; not reliably bred in captivity under typical hobby conditions.
